insight: The DD DDE DDC opening (Drone Drone, Drone Drone Engineer, Drone Drone Conduit) is considered a very poor opening line in most circumstances. Buying an Engineer commits you to a triple-Drone turn, but then pivoting to a Conduit wastes the Engineer's potential. mrguy888 states that if you play this sequence, you made a mistake on one of those three turns.
author: consensus
context: Discussion about a replay where a player opened DD DDE DDC, with amalloy and mrguy888 critiquing the incoherence of mixing Engineer and Conduit without a clear plan.

insight: Cauterizer functions primarily as soak (absorb), not as an offensive attacker. It can be used offensively only if you are already over the absorb barrier and plan to rebuild Engineers, otherwise Tarsier is the better offensive choice.
author: consensus
context: Review of a player's replay where they misused Cauterizer as an attacker instead of soak, with multiple players pointing out Tarsier's superior offensive efficiency.

insight: Plexo Cell makes prompt Engineers poor soak because Plexo provides its own defensive value; overbuilding prompt Engineers in a Plexo set is likely suboptimal compared to using Plexo's absorb directly.
author: f300xen
context: Set review discussion noting that prompt Engineers are bad soak when Plexo Cell is available in the set.

insight: When an opponent threatens a second Animus, the correct response may be to immediately purchase a Thunderhead rather than other units like Steelsplitter or Maharan Waste; delaying this response by being off-tempo (e.g. due to a Chrono Filter purchase) can be a losing mistake.
author: deadhour
context: Analysis of a game where a player failed to buy Thunderhead in time after opponent threatened a second Animus, with discussion of how Chrono Filter caused the player to be off-tempo.

insight: When fusing into a Tatsu Nullifier (an expensive tech unit requiring significant resource investment), the fusion must happen very early (turns 2-3) or it becomes a game-losing mistake due to the slow economy it implies. Fusing on turn 4 is already considered too slow.
author: consensus
context: Post-game review where amalloy and others noted that a turn 4 fuge into Tatsu was too slow, and that two Animuses would have been better given the economy available.

insight: Valkyrion is cost-efficient at approximately 2 permanent attack for 12 mana (attacking for 4 on the first turn then 2 each subsequent turn), comparable to Borehole Patroller's double-attack-on-first-turn mechanic. However, Valkyrion alone forces awkward tech requirements; it is best paired with synergistic units like Cryo Ray (granular chill to freeze barriers) or Thermite.
author: consensus
context: Players explaining Valkyrion's mechanics and synergies in response to a beginner asking how to use it.

insight: Arka (a high-absorb unit costing BBA) is itself an absorber that paradoxically denies absorb because it costs attack to counter. If an opponent goes counter-Arka early, they can deal only 1 damage into your Arka, effectively denying 5 of your absorb while your Arka only denied 2 absorb off a Wall. You should therefore wait to buy Arka until you can guarantee at least 3-4 absorb on it when the opponent mirrors.
author: cogito3
context: Detailed set analysis where Cogito explained why getting Arka first before having sufficient absorb is a strategic mistake.

insight: Arka tends to slow down sets significantly when there is no other large absorber available, because: (1) you need sufficient economy to support the BBA tech cost (similar to why Wincer requires 3 Engineers), and (2) you want to hit 7 attack roughly 0.5 turns after your opponent rather than 1.5 turns, requiring slightly more economy than them.
author: masn6811
context: Follow-up discussion after Cogito's Arka analysis, with masn6811 elaborating on why Arka is an economy-demanding unit even when it doesn't look like one.

insight: Zemora Voidbringer is not a burst unit despite its +8 attack; it is a slow, sustained-attack unit that takes ~6 turns to come online and requires significant Conduit investment that also does nothing immediately. It is better characterized as an economy-like unit. Fast sets with strong early attack are bad for Zemora; slower sets make it more viable.
author: consensus
context: Discussion triggered by a player asking about Ferritin Sac and Energy Matrix as support for a non-Zemora line, with multiple experts correcting the characterization of Zemora as a burst unit.

insight: A typical P1 Zemora line starts DD DC DDC, which comes out a full turn faster than the P2 version (DD DDC DDC), giving P1 a meaningful tempo advantage in Zemora mirror games.
author: amalloy
context: amalloy clarifying Zemora opening theory that was implied but not explicitly stated during a broader Zemora strategy discussion.

insight: Drake into double Lancetooth is a strong opening combination: Drake's first-turn bonus attack combined with double Lancetooth creates a dual threat of (a) large immediate damage and (b) absorb denial via Lancetooth, which is very difficult for the opponent to address. The 10-drone P1 version of this line is a well-established opening.
author: consensus
context: Discussion after amalloy was surprised by the Drake into double Lancetooth opening used against him, with mrguy888 confirming it is a known strong line.

insight: In Venge Cannon all-in lines, floating large amounts of green to make many Venges in a single turn is inefficient. A better approach is a slower burn: make Venges gradually while maintaining enough blue tech (only 5 gold to spend) to buy absorbers, then save up defense to absorb after running out of Drones. Having two Blastforges (e.g. with Grenade Mech available) reduces the effective tech cost in the long run.
author: mrguy888
context: mrguy888 providing detailed Venge all-in execution advice in response to a player asking when to go Venge all-in.

insight: Against Venge Cannon all-in, the correct defensive response is to attack the opponent's economy (Drones) rather than killing their Venges. Killing Iceblades instead of attacking their economy is a losing strategy according to mrguy888.
author: mrguy888
context: Discussion about Blood Pact Iceblade strategy where mrguy888 corrected a suggestion that you should focus on killing Iceblades rather than droning pressure.

insight: Gauss Cannon alone is insufficient to counter Iceblade Rush. To beat Iceblades you need to breach the opponent and kill their Drones (economy). Killing an Iceblade directly (e.g. on turn 8) is often a losing move; you must target their economy instead. In high-economy situations, depleting their Iceblade supply via breaching can work if you have sufficient soak.
author: shadourow
context: deadhour asking why their Gauss Cannon response to Iceblade Rush failed, with shadourow and siepu explaining the correct anti-Iceblade strategy.

insight: Cauterizer is much stronger when you actually threaten to fire it. Buying a Cauterizer with no plan to fire it implies you are in a situation where red mana is worth significantly less than one gold, making it a poor investment in that context.
author: amalloy
context: Discussion about when Cauterizer purchase is correct, with amalloy noting the unit's value depends heavily on credible threat of firing.

insight: Scorchilla functions like a superior Tarsier in regular sets: unlike standard first attackers, Scorchilla does not allow the opponent to absorb on the turn it is bought, while still providing attacker-level investment. In breachproof sets, Scorchilla costs effectively 6GG for 6 HP and two damage (with red being 'free'), but is vulnerable to being killed at half exhaust or on 1 exhaust, making it less dominant in that context.
author: mrguy888
context: Discussion about Scorchilla's role in breachproof versus regular sets, with mrguy888 defending its value against a claim it is below average.

insight: Tia Threnody functions as a third Engineer equivalent in terms of economy (BSO provides roughly 3 Engineers of value), but it is highly asymmetrical and forces the player to go to 16 Drones primarily as the only way to defend Tia's cost. A player with Tia should still expect a relatively fast game; calling Tia 'high econ' is misleading despite its 3-Engineer-equivalent output.
author: shadourow
context: shadourow clarifying the strategic characterization of Tia Threnody in response to a meme about high-econ units.

insight: In a set with Cynestra and Tia Threnody together, the pair provides good value/tempo. Against this combination, attempting to go big with Omega Splitter is wrong (a bad unit in this context); Centurion is the correct large unit to aim for if going big, but staying smallish with a turn-3 Conduit and mirroring the RG units (Cynestra) may be preferable.
author: amalloy
context: Set analysis where a player asked how to beat Cynestra, and amalloy evaluated the available large units and recommended mirroring or turn-3 Conduit play.

insight: Sacrificing a Wall to click a Rhino for attack is most justified when the Rhino's attack generates discrete value (e.g. killing a Centurion, turning off a Cauterizer). The trade-off is losing 3 HP of defense this turn while the opponent does 2 less damage; plus you kill an Engineer and regain the Rhino next turn. This is likely a net positive except in very high inflation situations.
author: amalloy
context: Discussion of when to sacrifice a Wall to activate Rhino's attack, triggered by a beginner's question about Wall vs Rhino trade-offs.

insight: In Prismata strategy discussion, 'reading the set' refers to identifying the correct strategic approach (attack type, economy level, tech path) from the available random units before or during the game. mrguy888 considers set reading the most important and most difficult skill in Prismata, even at the highest levels of play, and argues it cannot be shortcut by reading theory alone — pattern recognition from played games is essential.
author: mrguy888
context: A beginner asking for advice on reading sets, with mrguy888 and amalloy explaining why it is the hardest skill to teach.

insight: When considering a Shredder purchase to counter an anticipated Cynestra, the opponent can simply not buy the Cynestra and instead exploit the Shredder's investment by pushing to 4 damage and abusing that pressure. A Barrier already solves the defensive problem more safely, making the reactive Shredder purchase a risky over-commitment.
author: sano712
context: Post-game analysis where amalloy suggested a Shredder as a Cynestra counter and sano712 explained why it creates exploitable commitment.
